Snipara (RLM SaaS) is a "Context Optimization as a Service" platform. Core value propositions:

- ~90% context reduction — compresses large document sets (e.g., 500K tokens) to ~5K tokens of highly relevant content  
- Clients use their own LLM — no vendor lock-in and Snipara doesn't pass through LLM API costs  
- High margins — pure compute business model (no LLM inference costs)  
- LLM-agnostic — works with any MCP-compatible client

Key insight: the real value is the optimized context provided to the client's LLM (not the LLM response itself).

Snipara / RLM SaaS’s core value proposition:

- Context Optimization as a Service — deliver only the most relevant documentation/context to an LLM so queries fit token limits.
- ~90% context reduction (e.g., from ~500K tokens down to ~5K of highly relevant content).
- Customer uses their own LLM (no vendor lock‑in, no LLM API costs passed through).
- High margins (compute-only service) and LLM‑agnostic (works with any MCP‑compatible client).

Snipara has two pricing families — Context (context-optimization) plans and Agents (memory & swarms) plans.

- Context Plans:
  - FREE — $0, 100 queries/mo
  - PRO — $19/mo, 5,000 queries/mo
  - TEAM — $49/mo, 20,000 queries/mo
  - ENTERPRISE — $499/mo, Unlimited queries/mo

- Agents Plans (Memory & Swarms):
  - STARTER — $15/mo (no prerequisite)
  - PRO — $39/mo (no prerequisite)
  - TEAM — $79/mo (requires Context TEAM or higher)
  - ENTERPRISE — $199/mo (requires Context ENTERPRISE)

Note: Agents TEAM/ENTERPRISE are team-level features and require the corresponding Context plan.

Short summary:
Snipara is implemented across three repositories (two developer-facing + one enterprise mirror). One of them is the production source for the MCP server; the others are a PyPI client package and an enterprise/self-hosted mirror that must be kept in sync.

Components
- alopez3006/snipara-webapp (monorepo) — primary development repo
  - Contains apps/web (Next.js web app), apps/mcp-server (Python FastAPI MCP server), and packages/database (Prisma schema + JS client).
  - Source of truth for production deploys; apps/mcp-server is the code published to Railway as the production MCP service.
  - Production Dockerfile (apps/mcp-server/Dockerfile) does not run prisma db push at runtime.

- alopez3006/snipara-mcp — PyPI client package
  - Standalone git repo that packages the MCP client for PyPI.
  - NOT deployed as a server; used to distribute the Python client library.

- Snipara/snipara-server — enterprise / self-hosted mirror
  - Local/enterprise clone used for self-hosted deployments and enterprise releases.
  - NOT the production deployment; must be manually synced from the monorepo after MCP changes.
  - Contains init-db.sh that runs prisma db push --accept-data-loss (dangerous if schema is stale).

Production details & important operational notes
- Production MCP service: api.snipara.com is deployed from apps/mcp-server in the monorepo (Railway service snipara-fastapi).
- Manual deploy command: cd apps/mcp-server && railway up -d -s "snipara-fastapi" -e "production".
- Prisma schema sync is critical:
  - packages/database/prisma/schema.prisma (JS) is the source of truth.
  - After JS schema changes: run pnpm db:push and pnpm db:generate, then copy new models/fields/enums to apps/mcp-server/prisma/schema.prisma (generator set to prisma-client-py) before redeploying MCP server.
  - If the Python schema is stale, enterprise init-db.sh (which uses --accept-data-loss) can DROP columns. Keep schemas in sync.

Files that must be synced to the enterprise repo when changing MCP/server logic
- server.py, db.py, auth.py, rlm_engine.py, mcp_transport.py, usage.py, config.py, models.py, services/*.py, prisma/schema.prisma

Risk notes (from the docs)
- Never add a Railway startCommand override for snipara-fastapi (it would force single-worker uvicorn and cause zombie replicas).
- Pre-download and preload embedding models in Docker build to avoid cold-starts and blocked workers.
- Follow the documented sync steps carefully to avoid data loss from prisma db push in the enterprise mirror.

Short answer:
- Token budgeting is controlled by the Snipara MCP tools (primarily rlm_context_query). You request a max_tokens budget and Snipara searches, ranks, compresses/chunks, and returns only the most relevant sections such that the returned context fits that token budget.

How it works (details):
- Caller-controlled budget
  - rlm_context_query accepts max_tokens and will return optimized context sized to that limit. Example: rlm_context_query(query="auth", max_tokens=6000).
  - Projects have a stored default maxTokensPerQuery (default 4000) that applies when you don’t override it.
  - rlm_plan / rlm_decompose may return suggested token budgets per sub-step for multi-step work.

- LITE vs FULL workflow budgets
  - LITE tasks: ~3–5K token budgets (most quick edits).
  - FULL tasks: ~8–15K token budgets (multi-file / architectural work).

- Shared context + project context split
  - When shared/team context is included, token budget is split between project docs and shared context. Typical distribution: 60% project docs + 40% shared context.
  - Shared collections themselves use a category-based allocation within that portion: MANDATORY 40% / BEST_PRACTICES 30% / GUIDELINES 20% / REFERENCE 10% (these percentages are used to allocate tokens across categories when loading shared docs).

- Budget-aware selection and compression
  - Snipara ranks candidate sections by relevance and uses per-section token counts (DocumentChunk.tokenCount, DocumentSummary.tokenCount) to pack the highest-value content into the budget.
  - It may compress/summarize or chunk content so the most useful material fits the requested token budget rather than returning raw full documents.

- Operational safeguards that affect budgeting
  - There are hard caps/thresholds in search (e.g., candidate caps, stop-word/title-only fallbacks) to keep semantic search and embedding work within real-world time/budget limits.
  - For very large on-the-fly embedding jobs, Snipara may use a faster fallback embedding model to avoid long timeouts; this affects which candidates get considered for the budgeted response.

Practical notes / examples:
- To get compact context for a quick edit: rlm_context_query("fix login bug", max_tokens=3000) → returns ~3K tokens of optimized context.
- For a multi-chunk plan step: rlm_context_query(chunk_query, max_tokens=6000) (FULL workflow recommended).
- Use rlm_shared_context(categories=[...], max_tokens=4000) to explicitly load shared rules (the tool returns budget_allocation so you can see how tokens were used).

What’s tracked
- Token counts are tracked per chunk/summary and in query caches so future queries can reuse cached, budget-aware results.

Short answer
- Snipara enforces a per-query token budget: rlm tools select, rank and compress/document-chunk content so the returned "OPTIMIZED CONTEXT" fits within the requested token budget (you pass max_tokens or use a tool default).
- Use rlm_context_query for full, budgeted context (configurable max_tokens). Use rlm_ask for quick predictable replies (~2500 tokens).
- LITE vs FULL modes have recommended budgets: LITE ≈ 3–5K tokens; FULL ≈ 8–15K tokens (chosen via the mode-scoring rules in WORKFLOW_MODES).

How it works (from the docs)
- Query + budget: rlm_context_query (and related planning tools) take a max_tokens parameter. Snipara performs search → rank → score → then compresses/chunks the highest‑ranked sections so the sum of returned sections fits the token budget.
- Shared context + project split: when team/shared context is included, Snipara distributes the budget between project docs and shared context (docs state a 60% project / 40% shared split by default). Shared context itself is budgeted by category: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10% (shown in rlm_shared_context output as budget_allocation).
- Tool guidance: rlm_plan and rlm_decompose will suggest context budgets per execution step (useful in FULL workflows). rlm_ask is a quick tool with a fixed/predictable budget (~2500 tokens).
- Token-aware storage: documents and chunks include token counts (e.g., DocumentChunk.tokenCount, DocumentSummary.tokenCount) so selection/pruning respects token accounting.
- Project defaults: projects have a maxTokensPerQuery field (default 4000) and Snipara honors project configuration unless overridden per-call.

Practical notes / recommendations
- For quick edits/bug fixes use LITE (rlm_context_query with ~3–5K budget). For multi‑chunk, architectural work use FULL and let rlm_plan/rlm_decompose allocate larger budgets.
- Explicitly pass max_tokens when you need more/less context. If you rely on team standards, load rlm_shared_context first (it returns the category budget allocation) and inject it into session so rlm_context_query includes it automatically.
- Remember: Snipara returns optimized context (not the LLM response). Keep your client's downstream LLM token limit in mind when choosing the Snipara budget.

Short summary
- Snipara treats the context you pass to your LLM as a fixed token budget and returns a compressed, ranked set of sections that fit that budget (it does search → rank → score → compress/chunk).
- You control budget via the tool params (rlm_context_query max_tokens, rlm_plan suggestions, project default maxTokensPerQuery); there are also preset workflows (LITE vs FULL) with recommended budgets.

How the budgeting works (step‑by‑step)
1. Query + candidate selection
   - rlm_context_query performs semantic/keyword (hybrid) search to find candidate sections across project docs and any linked shared-context collections.
   - There is a performance/candidate cap (on-the-fly embedding path uses a fast fallback model and a hard cap on candidates — documented as 30 candidates — plus stop‑word/title filters and basic stemming to keep results focused).

2. Ranking & scoring
   - Candidates are ranked by relevance scores (semantic + keyword heuristics, shared‑context filters).
   - Shared context documents are filtered so non-MANDATORY docs don’t outrank project content.

3. Budget allocation (how tokens are split)
   - The tool enforces the max_tokens value you pass (or project default: maxTokensPerQuery = 4000).
   - When shared/team context is included, Snipara splits the budget between project docs and shared context. Example in docs: token budget distributed 60% project docs / 40% shared context.
   - Within shared context, budget is further split by category priority: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10% (the rlm_shared_context response shows this allocation as numeric token counts).

4. Compression / chunking / truncation
   - After ranking, Snipara compresses (summarizes / chunks) sections to fit the allocated token budget. Document chunks and summaries have precomputed token counts (document_chunks.tokenCount, document_summaries.tokenCount) used for precise budget accounting.
   - The system will prefer higher‑ranked content and MANDATORY shared docs when trimming content.

5. Outputs & tooling
   - rlm_context_query returns the optimized context (sections) sized to your max_tokens.
   - rlm_plan and rlm_decompose will return suggested context budgets per step for complex, multi‑chunk work.
   - Quick tool rlm_ask provides a smaller, predictable budget (~2500 tokens) for fast queries.

Practical defaults / recommendations from the docs
- LITE mode (most tasks): token budget ≈ 3–5K (use rlm_context_query for current chunk).
- FULL mode (complex features): token budget ≈ 8–15K (rlm_plan → rlm_decompose → rlm_context_query with larger max_tokens).
- Project default maxTokensPerQuery = 4000 (can be overridden per request).
- rlm_ask is a quick ~2500 token query shortcut.

Implementation details the system relies on
- Precomputed token counts on chunks/summaries to track and allocate tokens precisely.
- Dual‑model embedding strategy (large model for precomputed indexing; smaller fallback for on‑the‑fly embedding) to meet latency/timeout constraints.
- Candidate caps and stop‑word/title filters to avoid irrelevant context consuming the budget.

How to control it as a user
- Set max_tokens in rlm_context_query to your desired budget.
- For complex features, use rlm_plan/rlm_decompose so Snipara suggests per‑step budgets.
- Use rlm_shared_context to fetch team standards first (you can inject that into session) — Snipara will factor shared context into the token split automatically.

Short answer
- Shared context collections are team- or global-level bundles of documentation (coding standards, best practices, templates, guidelines, reference material) that projects can inherit so every project/query automatically sees the team's rules and patterns.
- They’re used to enforce mandatory rules (security/architecture), surface best practices, provide templates, and keep implementations consistent across projects.

How they work (workflow)
1. Create or find a collection
   - List collections: rlm_list_collections(include_public=true)
   - Collections can be GLOBAL, TEAM, or USER scoped; public collections (React, Python, Security, etc.) are available too.
2. Upload documents into a collection
   - rlm_upload_shared_document(collection_id=..., title=..., content=..., category=..., priority=...)
   - Documents are categorized: MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE and given a priority (0–100) within the category.
3. Link a collection to projects
   - Linking is done from the dashboard (projects inherit documents from linked collections).
   - Under the hood this creates ProjectSharedContext records; you can set tokenBudgetPercent, enabledCategories, and priority per link to override defaults.
4. Consumption during queries & development
   - rlm_shared_context(...) returns team standards (you can fetch categories and max_tokens).
   - When a project runs rlm_context_query(...), Snipara automatically includes linked shared context in results. Token budgets are distributed (example: docs show a 60% project docs + 40% shared context split).
   - You can inject shared context into the session with rlm_inject(...) so it applies to all subsequent queries/implementations.
5. Prompt templates & tooling
   - Collections can include prompt templates (rlm_list_templates, rlm_get_template).
   - Snipara tools automatically apply collection rules when generating plans, decompositions, or when RLM-Runtime runs code.

Key behaviors & policies
- Category priority & token allocation: typical budget allocation shown in docs is MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10% (Snipara will allocate tokens across those categories when assembling context).
- Document priority (0–100) determines ordering within a category.
- Projects inherit linked collections automatically; token allocation and category filters can be overridden per project link.
- Shared context helps ensure implementations follow mandatory rules (e.g., Zod validation), recommended patterns, and team conventions automatically.
- Access: Shared Collections are a Team/Enterprise feature (available on Team plan and up).

Benefits (from docs)
- Team-wide, reusable standards
- Automatic inclusion in context queries (so client LLM sees team rules)
- Reduce duplicated effort and inconsistent implementations
- Template reuse (code-review, PR templates, etc.)

Practical examples (from docs)
- Upload team standards:
  rlm_upload_shared_document(
    collection_id="team_collection_id",
    title="TypeScript Coding Standards",
    content="...",
    category="BEST_PRACTICES",
    priority=90
  )
- Load standards before work:
  shared = rlm_shared_context(categories=["MANDATORY","BEST_PRACTICES"], max_tokens=4000)
- Inject to session:
  rlm_inject(context=f"Team Coding Standards:\n{shared['documents'][0]['content']}", append=False)
- Now rlm_context_query("implement user registration endpoint") will include those standards automatically.

Where to read more
- docs/SHARED_CONTEXT.md (full shared context docs)
- docs/MCP_TOOLS_COMPLETE.md for tool parameter details (rlm_shared_context, rlm_upload_shared_document, templates)

Short answer
- Shared context collections are reusable sets of documentation (coding standards, best practices, guidelines, prompt templates, MCP defaults) that teams or the platform publish and make automatically available to projects.
- They let teams enforce and share mandatory rules and recommended patterns so Snipara returns team-aligned context with every project query.

How they work (steps & behavior)
1. Create & populate a collection
   - Team admins create a Shared Context Collection and upload documents (rlm_upload_shared_document). Each document has:
     - category (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE)
     - priority (0–100) to rank importance within a category
     - token count / content (used for budgeting)
   - Collections can be TEAM-scoped, USER-scoped, or GLOBAL/public.

2. Link collections to projects
   - Collections are linked to projects in the dashboard (ProjectSharedContext). Projects inherit all documents from enabled collections automatically.

3. Token budgeting & ranking
   - When a query runs, Snipara distributes the token budget between project docs and shared context (example: 60% project docs / 40% shared context).
   - Within shared context, budget is further allocated by category priority (example allocation shown in docs: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%).
   - Documents are ranked by category, priority, relevance, and token budget to decide which sections to return.

4. Querying & injection
   - Use rlm_shared_context(...) to fetch team standards (filter by categories, set max_tokens).
   - Shared context is automatically included by rlm_context_query for project queries; you can also inject it into a session with rlm_inject(...) so it applies to all subsequent queries.
   - Prompt templates in collections are available via rlm_list_templates / rlm_get_template.

5. Config & tooling
   - Collections can include SharedMcpConfig to control default tool parameters (enabled tools, default max_tokens, default search mode).
   - Team plans are required to create/manage private shared collections; public collections are available to all.

Best practices & recommendations
- Put non-negotiable rules in MANDATORY documents (security, architecture).
- Use BEST_PRACTICES for recommended patterns (tests, style).
- Keep documents focused (one topic per doc) and set sensible priorities.

Short answer
- Shared context collections are reusable sets of documentation (coding standards, best practices, templates, guidelines, reference material) that a team or organization can create once and have automatically applied to all linked projects.
- They let Snipara include team-wide rules and guidance into context queries so implementations automatically follow team standards without copying docs into each project.

How they work (concise)
1. Create a collection (team admin): upload focused documents to a Shared Context Collection (rlm_upload_shared_document). Collections can be scoped (GLOBAL / TEAM / USER) and marked public/official.
2. Categorize & prioritize: each shared document is assigned a category and priority:
   - Categories (priority ordering): MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE.
   - Priority (0–100) orders importance within a category.
3. Link collections to projects: via the dashboard (or ProjectSharedContext entries). Linked projects automatically inherit documents from their collections.
4. Token budgeting & inclusion:
   - Snipara splits query token budget between project docs and shared context (docs state: default distribution ≈ 60% project docs + 40% shared context).
   - Within shared context the token budget is allocated by category (example allocation shown by rlm_shared_context: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%).
5. Runtime behavior:
   - When you run rlm_context_query for a project, Snipara automatically includes shared context from linked collections (filtered by category/priority and token budget).
   - You can explicitly retrieve shared standards up-front with rlm_shared_context (useful at session start).
   - You can inject shared context into the session with rlm_inject so subsequent queries and tools see the standards without re-fetching.
6. Supporting features:
   - Prompt templates and MCP configs can live inside collections (rlm_list_templates / rlm_get_template).
   - Collections expose metadata (isPublic, isOfficial, version, usageCount) and can have SharedMcpConfig to control tool defaults.

Typical workflow (example)
- Team admin: rlm_list_collections → rlm_upload_shared_document(collection_id=..., category="BEST_PRACTICES", priority=90, content=...)
- Developer session start: shared = rlm_shared_context(categories=["MANDATORY","BEST_PRACTICES"], max_tokens=4000)
- Inject for session: rlm_inject(context=shared['documents'][0]['content'], append=False)
- Project query: rlm_context_query(query="implement user registration", max_tokens=6000) — Snipara returns project sections plus shared context (token-split applied), so your implementation follows team rules.

Why use them
- Ensure non-negotiable rules (security/architecture) are always considered
- Enforce best practices automatically without manual reminders
- Reduce duplicate documentation and centralize standards for many projects
- Provide reusable prompt templates and MCP config for consistent tooling

- rlm_shared_context, rlm_inject, rlm_context_query
- rlm_list_templates, rlm_get_template

Summary (how it works)

- Start: client requests a device code (POST /oauth/device/code). Snipara creates a DeviceCode record with a long deviceCode and a short userCode, an expiresAt, a polling interval (default 5s) and scope (default "mcp:read mcp:write").
- User auth: the user visits the verification/authorize UI (out-of-band), types the userCode (or follows the provided verification URL) and signs in to approve the device. The DeviceCode status moves from PENDING → AUTHORIZED and authorizedAt / authorizedById are set. Device codes can also be DENIED, EXPIRED or USED.
- Client polling: the device client repeatedly polls the token endpoint (using the deviceCode) at the configured interval waiting for authorization. On success the server issues tokens (access and optionally refresh).
- Tokens: issued OAuth tokens are persisted in the oauth_tokens table as hashed values (accessTokenHash, refreshTokenHash). A tokenPrefix (first 12 chars) is kept for display/identification. Records include clientId/clientName, projectId (optional), scopes, accessExpiresAt and refreshExpiresAt, lastUsedAt and revokedAt.
- Use: clients present the bearer token in requests as Authorization: Bearer <snipara_at_...> (MCP calls expect this header).
- Plugin/auto-provision: DeviceCode has an autoProvision flag to support plugin auto-registration flows (device code can be bound to a project/client on approval).

Important implementation details / gotchas from the docs

- Poll interval: device polling uses the DeviceCode.interval (default 5s).
- Status lifecycle: PENDING → AUTHORIZED → (token exchange → mark USED); can become EXPIRED or DENIED if not approved.
- Security: tokens are stored as hashes (so the DB doesn’t keep raw tokens).
- Scopes: default scope is "mcp:read mcp:write" unless otherwise requested.
- Bindings: DeviceCode can include a projectId so the resulting token can be scoped to a project.
- Refresh tokens: supported (refreshTokenHash and refreshExpiresAt are stored).
- API POST redirect gotcha: do NOT call POST endpoints at snipara.com (which 301→www.snipara.com). That redirect can convert POST→GET and lead to 405 errors — always call the API POST endpoints at www.snipara.com.

How the token is used by MCP clients

- After exchange, use the access token in the Authorization header for MCP/API requests (Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...).
- The MCP server validates tokens against oauth_tokens (hash lookup), enforces expiry/revocation, and records lastUsedAt for the token.

Short summary
- Snipara implements the OAuth 2.0 device authorization (device flow) so CLIs, plugins and headless clients can get an access token by having the user approve on a separate browser-capable device.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. Client requests a device code (endpoint: POST /oauth/device/code). The server creates a DeviceCode record with:
   - deviceCode (long random code) and userCode (short human code)
   - projectId (optional), scope (e.g. "mcp:read mcp:write")
   - status = PENDING, interval (polling interval, default 5s), expiresAt, autoProvision flag
2. Client shows the user the userCode and instructs them to visit the authorization page on a browser-capable device and enter the code.
3. User signs in/approves in the browser. On approval the DeviceCode row is updated: status → AUTHORIZED, authorizedAt and authorizedById are set (and project association is applied if needed).
4. Meanwhile the client polls the server (at the DeviceCode.interval, default 5s) asking whether the deviceCode has been authorized. Polling continues until:
   - status = AUTHORIZED → server issues tokens
   - status = EXPIRED / DENIED → flow fails
5. When authorized the server creates an OAuthToken record and returns tokens to the client. Notes about storage and fields:
   - access and refresh tokens are stored as hashes in the DB (accessTokenHash, refreshTokenHash)
   - tokenPrefix (first characters) is saved for identification
   - OAuthToken includes clientName, scope, accessExpiresAt, refreshExpiresAt, lastUsedAt, revokedAt, createdAt
   - tokens may be scoped to a projectId if provided
6. Use the returned access token for API/MCP calls with:
   - Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_... (device-flow OAuth bearer token)
   - (Project-scoped API keys use X-API-Key: rlm_... — different method)

Important enums / fields to know
- DeviceCode.status values: PENDING, AUTHORIZED, EXPIRED, USED, DENIED
- Default polling interval in DeviceCode: interval = 5 (seconds)
- OAuthToken stored fields: accessTokenHash, refreshTokenHash, tokenPrefix, accessExpiresAt, refreshExpiresAt, lastUsedAt, revokedAt

Practical notes / troubleshooting
- The client should poll at the configured interval and stop when expiresAt or a terminal status is reached.
- For POST requests to the API, always use www.snipara.com (not bare snipara.com). A 301 redirect from snipara.com → www.snipara.com can turn a POST into a GET and cause device‑flow POSTs to fail with 405.
- The DB only stores token hashes for security; tokenPrefix and timestamps let you identify and manage tokens (lastUsedAt, revokedAt).

Extras
- DeviceCode.autoProvision can be used for plugin/auto-provisioning scenarios.
- DeviceCodes may include an optional projectId so issued OAuthTokens can be project-scoped.

Short answer — device flow in Snipara is the standard OAuth device-authorize pattern plus a few product specifics:

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. Client requests a device code: POST /oauth/device/code → server creates a DeviceCode record (status=PENDING) and returns deviceCode, userCode, expiresAt, interval (default interval = 5s) and any verification URL. The DeviceCode may include projectId, clientId, clientName and autoProvision flags.
2. User authorizes: the user visits the verification URL (or copies userCode into the UI) and approves the requested scopes for the project. The server marks the DeviceCode row AUTHORIZED (authorizedAt, authorizedById).
3. Client polls for tokens: the device/client polls the token exchange endpoint at the specified interval using the deviceCode. When the DeviceCode status becomes AUTHORIZED the server issues tokens.
4. Tokens stored securely: access/refresh tokens are stored as hashes in the OAuthToken table (accessTokenHash, refreshTokenHash). A tokenPrefix (first ~12 chars) is kept for identification, and OAuthToken rows include userId, projectId, scope, accessExpiresAt, refreshExpiresAt, lastUsedAt, revokedAt, createdAt.
5. Use tokens: clients include the access token in requests (Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...) to call MCP APIs / tools.
6. Status lifecycle: DeviceCode.status values are PENDING, AUTHORIZED, EXPIRED, USED, DENIED. Tokens likewise can be revoked (revokedAt) and tracked via lastUsedAt.

Important implementation details / gotchas from the docs
- The DeviceCode model fields: deviceCode, userCode, projectId (optional), scope (default "mcp:read mcp:write"), clientId, status, authorizedAt, authorizedById, autoProvision, expiresAt, interval (default 5).
- OAuthToken model stores token hashes (accessTokenHash, refreshTokenHash) and tokenPrefix, and records accessExpiresAt, refreshExpiresAt, lastUsedAt, revokedAt.
- Security/storage: tokens are stored hashed (only prefixes kept for display/lookup).
- Critical networking gotcha: always use www.snipara.com for API POST endpoints — snipara.com performs a 301 → www which will convert POST→GET and can silently break device flow (405). Use the www host for POSTs.
- Auto-provision: DeviceCode.autoProvision can be used for plugin/auto-provisioning flows (client registration behavior is recorded on the DeviceCode).
- Polling interval: default interval is 5s (stored on DeviceCode) and should be respected to avoid rate limits.

Snipara uses a strict layered (n-tier) architecture. Key points:

- Layer order (top → bottom): Route Handlers (thin) → Auth Layer → Service Layer (orchestration/business logic) → Query/Repository Layer (one file per entity, pure Prisma calls) → Database (Postgres + pgvector).
- Separation/enforcement:
  - Route handlers are thin (max ~50 lines); services handle orchestration.
  - Services are limited in size (max ~100 lines).
  - Query files are small (max ~50 lines) and contain only Prisma DB calls.
  - Lower layers MUST NOT import higher layers (one-way dependencies).
- Implementation mapping:
  - Web app: app/api/**/route.ts → lib/auth → lib/services/** → lib/db/queries/** → @snipara/database (Prisma client).
  - MCP server: server.py/JSON-RPC → auth.py → rlm_engine.py / services/** → db.py (async Prisma client) → Neon Postgres.
- Purpose: keep route handlers thin, centralize business logic in services, isolate DB access to a repository layer, and enforce strict layering for maintainability and testability.

- Route handlers (thin): Next.js/FastAPI routes kept minimal (max ~50 lines) — just auth, orchestration, and response.
- Auth layer: Session/API-key validation, permission and ban checks.
- Service layer: Orchestration and business logic (domain services, Stripe/GitHub integration, search/orchestration). Services are short and focused.

Short answer
- Use rlm_context_query for normal documentation/code lookups and to fetch the optimized context you need to implement or answer a task.
- Use rlm_decompose when a task is complex and you need to split it into ordered implementation chunks (FULL-mode work): it breaks a large plan/query into sub-queries with dependencies.

When to use each (details)
- rlm_context_query
  - Primary, first-line tool for documentation queries.
  - Use for: “How does X work?”, single-file changes, bug fixes, or any time you need relevant sections of docs/code (LITE mode).
  - Features: semantic + keyword search, token budgeting, returns ranked sections you pass to your LLM.
  - Example (from docs):
    context = rlm_context_query(
      query="<current chunk task>",
      max_tokens=6000,
      search_mode="hybrid"
    )

- rlm_decompose
  - Power‑user tool to break a complex implementation or plan into manageable chunks/sub-queries.
  - Use for: multi-file features, architectural changes, multi-session work — anytime the task scores 2+ by the LITE vs FULL checklist (multiple sessions, affects 5+ files, architecture decisions, documentation for others).
  - Typical flow: generate a plan (rlm_plan) → decompose it (rlm_decompose) → iterate per chunk using rlm_context_query.
  - Returns: sub-queries, dependencies, suggested execution order.
  - Example (from docs):
    chunks = rlm_decompose(
      query="OAuth 2.0 integration implementation",
      max_depth=2
    )

Recommended sequence for FULL work
1. rlm_plan(...) to create an execution plan (relevance_first / depth_first, etc.)
2. rlm_decompose(plan_query, max_depth=...) to split the plan into chunks
3. For each chunk: rlm_context_query(chunk["query"], max_tokens=...) to fetch optimized context
4. Implement & test chunk-by-chunk (use RLM-Runtime), then rlm_remember decisions

Quick rules of thumb
- Quick doc lookup or single-step implementation → rlm_context_query.
- Need to split work into ordered subtasks, handle dependencies, or run multi-day/multi-file work → rlm_decompose (after rlm_plan or as part of FULL-mode planning).
- Always try Snipara MCP tools first (docs’ non-negotiable rule).

If you want, I can show a short example workflow (plan → decompose → per-chunk context queries) using the exact calls from the docs.

Short answer
- Use rlm_context_query when you need optimized, token-budgeted documentation/context for a single question or implementation step (semantic + keyword search → return sections to feed your LLM or to code against).
- Use rlm_decompose when the work is complex and must be broken into smaller implementable sub‑tasks (multi-file, multi-session, architectural) — typically after you generate a plan (rlm_plan) in FULL mode.

When to pick which (rules of thumb)
- rlm_context_query
  - Single-question lookups: “How does authentication work?” or “Find the login handler.”
  - Small features, bug fixes, or a single chunk of work (LITE mode).
  - You want the actual optimized context (sections + token budgeting) to pass into your LLM or RLM-Runtime.
  - Example use from docs:
    context = rlm_context_query(query="<current chunk task>", max_tokens=6000, search_mode="hybrid")

- rlm_decompose
  - Complex features that should be split into chunks: multi-file changes, architectural decisions, multi-session work (FULL mode).
  - You want sub-queries, dependencies, a suggested execution order, and chunk granularity control (max_depth).
  - Typically used after rlm_plan to turn the plan into implementable chunks for per-chunk iteration with rlm_context_query + RLM-Runtime.
  - Example use from docs:
    rlm_decompose(query="<feature implementation task>", max_depth=2)
  - Output: sub-queries, dependencies, suggested sequence (used to iterate chunk-by-chunk).

Workflow guidance (recommended)
1. If this is a documentation/code query: try rlm_context_query first (this is a non‑negotiable rule).
2. If the task scores 2+ on the FULL-mode checklist (multi-session, 5+ files, architectural, needs docs for others), run rlm_plan → rlm_decompose to get chunks, then for each chunk call rlm_context_query and implement with RLM-Runtime.
3. Use max_depth on rlm_decompose to control chunk granularity; use rlm_context_query per chunk to get the token-budgeted context.

Bottom line: rlm_context_query = get context for one step. rlm_decompose = split a complex task into many such steps.

Short answer
- Use rlm_context_query to fetch relevant documentation/context for a single question or implementation chunk.
- Use rlm_decompose to break a large/complex task into smaller sub‑queries/chunks (power‑user / FULL workflows) before you query context for each chunk.

When to pick which (rules of thumb)
- rlm_context_query
  - Simple doc lookups, feature questions, single‑session changes, or when you already know the scope/file.
  - LITE mode work (bug fixes, small features): typical token budget ~3–5K.
  - Also used repeatedly inside FULL workflows to get context for each implementation chunk (examples use max_tokens=6000–8000).
  - First tool to call for “How does X work?” queries — try this before any fallbacks.
- rlm_decompose
  - Power‑user tool for complex features that need planning and multiple implementation steps.
  - Use after generating a plan (rlm_plan) to split the plan into independent, ordered sub‑queries/chunks with dependencies.
  - Appropriate when the task spans many files, requires architectural decisions, or will take multiple sessions (i.e., FULL mode).

Recommended flow (complex feature)
1. rlm_plan(...) → high‑level execution plan.
2. rlm_decompose(...) → produce chunks/sub‑queries and dependencies.
3. For each chunk: context = rlm_context_query(chunk_query, max_tokens=...) → implement + test.
4. rlm_remember(...) to record decisions / progress.

Quick examples
- “Fix auth bug in single file” → rlm_context_query("auth bug ...") (LITE).
- “Implement OAuth 2.0 integration across backend + frontend” → rlm_plan(...), rlm_decompose(...), then per chunk use rlm_context_query(...), RLM-Runtime for implementation (FULL).

Tip
- For very fast, lower‑budget lookups use rlm_ask (~2.5K tokens) — but prefer rlm_context_query for full‑featured semantic + budgeted results.

Short answer: use "keyword" for exact / syntactic lookups, "semantic" for conceptual / paraphrase queries, and "hybrid" for general-purpose queries (best balance — default). Use rlm_search (regex) when you know the file/path/identifier and need exact text matches.

Guidance and examples
- hybrid — Default for most queries
  - When to use: general troubleshooting, feature questions, “how does X work?”, or when you want both exact matches and conceptually relevant sections.
  - Why: combines keyword precision + semantic recall so you get relevant passages plus exact hits.
- semantic — Conceptual / paraphrase / discovery queries
  - When to use: “Explain OAuth flow”, “find examples of retry/backoff”, or when wording may differ from docs.
  - Why: embedding-based matching surfaces paraphrases and related concepts.
  - Caution: semantic embedding work is heavier and may be slower on very large corpora — MCP uses a fallback model and caps candidates, so narrow your scope if you hit timeouts.
- keyword — Exact / syntactic lookups
  - When to use: function/class names, config keys, error strings, filenames, logs, or when you need deterministic exact matches.
  - Why: fast, low-cost, avoids embedding overhead and is robust for large document sets.
- rlm_search / regex — Precise pattern or file-level search
  - When to use: you know the path or regex (e.g., “find .env keys”, grep-like searches), or you need line/character-level matches.
  - Why: designed for pattern search; use it when no semantic relevance is needed.

Operational tips
- Default to rlm_context_query with search_mode="hybrid" for most work (the project `searchMode` supports "keyword" | "semantic" | "hybrid").
- If you expect many candidate sections (600+), prefer keyword or restrict scope because on-the-fly semantic embedding of 600+ chunks can time out; MCP falls back to a smaller, faster model but results/latency vary.
- For very quick/small queries, use rlm_ask (quick, predictable token budget) or rlm_search for exact text.
- When working in LITE vs FULL workflows: LITE (small, known files) → keyword/rlm_search; FULL (broad design/architecture questions) → hybrid or semantic as part of a planned rlm_context_query with larger token budget.

Quick recommendation
- Start with hybrid. If results are too noisy or you need strict exactness, switch to keyword/rlm_search. If results miss paraphrased content, switch to semantic.

Short answer — use:

- hybrid — Default / general-purpose. Best first try for most questions (implementation how-tos, “how does X work”, code + docs) because it combines keyword precision with semantic recall. Many examples and project defaults use search_mode="hybrid" in rlm_context_query.

- semantic — Use for conceptual / paraphrased queries where wording varies (design rationale, “why”, cross-file concepts, finding patterns described differently). Note: semantic uses embeddings and can be slower on very large scans (the MCP server uses a fast fallback and caps candidates for huge inputs), so increase budget only when needed.

- keyword (regex) — Use when you need exact matches: filenames, specific code patterns, config keys. For code-pattern searches prefer rlm_search (regex) or local grep/Read when you already know the file path.

Practical tips from the docs:
- Always try rlm_context_query (hybrid) first for documentation/code queries. If the MCP API errors/timeout, fall back to local Read/Grep/Glob tools.
- For quick/small queries use rlm_ask (~2.5K token budget).
- For cross-project discovery use rlm_multi_project_query.
- If semantic queries appear slow or time out (very large projects / 600+ sections), try increasing token budget, use hybrid, or rely on keyword/regex searches and cached results.

Short answer
- Hybrid: default for most queries — best balance of recall and precision.
- Semantic: use when you need meaning-based matches (concepts, docs, design, ambiguous language).
- Keyword: use when you need exact string/title/path matches or very fast, deterministic results.
- rlm_search: use for regex / precise code-pattern searches.
- rlm_ask / low-budget: use when you want a quick, small-budget query (~2500 tokens).

When to pick each (with examples)
- Hybrid (recommended for general use)
  - When: most documentation / implementation queries, e.g., “how does authentication work?”, “implement user registration API endpoint”
  - Why: combines keyword signals with semantic ranking; used as the project default (project.searchMode = "hybrid") and in examples.
  - Example call: rlm_context_query(query="authentication", search_mode="hybrid")

- Semantic
  - When: conceptual, paraphrased, or vague queries where wording differs from source (design rationale, best‑practice lookups, “how should we…”, feature intent).
  - Why: finds meaning-level matches rather than exact tokens.
  - Caveat: semantic embedding work is heavier — on very large scans (hundreds of chunks) on‑the‑fly embedding can approach timeouts; the MCP server uses a dual-model fallback for heavy batches.

- Keyword
  - When: you know exact identifiers, filenames, headings, config keys, or need deterministic matches (e.g., "NEXTAUTH_SECRET", "apps/mcp-server/src/auth.py", "## OAuth 2.0").
  - Why: fast and precise; useful for exact code/config lookups.
  - Also: If you need regex / pattern matching across files, prefer rlm_search (the MCP regex tool) instead of keyword semantic search.

- rlm_search (regex)
  - When: precise code pattern searches, grep-style lookups, or searching by regular expression.
  - Example: find occurrences of a function signature or specific import pattern.

- rlm_ask / quick queries
  - When: you want a fast, low‑budget (~2500 token) context query without extra configuration (small quick checks).

Practical tips & caveats
- Default to hybrid for 90% of tasks — it’s the documented example/default and balances relevance + speed.
- Use semantic for deep conceptual matching; use keyword/rlm_search for exact code or path lookups.
- For very large projects or queries that touch 600+ sections, semantic on-the-fly embedding can be slow; prefer hybrid or keyword, or rely on cached/precomputed chunks (MCP handles dual-model fallbacks).
- If you already know the file path, just Read that file locally (Snipara vs local rules): reading a known file is a local-tool case — no search needed.
- If the MCP API errors or times out, fall back to local Read/Grep/Glob per the workflow rules.

Short answer — token budget is split between project docs and shared context, then distributed across shared categories by fixed percentages.

Details:
- Default split: 60% of the query token budget is used for project documents, 40% is reserved for shared context collections. Snipara automatically includes shared context when you call rlm_context_query.
- Within the shared 40%, tokens are allocated by category (priority order):
  - MANDATORY = 40% of the shared budget
  - BEST_PRACTICES = 30% of the shared budget
  - GUIDELINES = 20% of the shared budget
  - REFERENCE = 10% of the shared budget
- You can call rlm_shared_context(categories=[...], max_tokens=...) to preload shared docs (it returns documents plus a budget_allocation mapping). rlm_context_query(max_tokens=...) will incorporate shared context according to the above split.
- Selection inside each shared category respects document priority (the shared_documents.priority field) and token counts — documents are chosen to fill the category’s token bucket.

Example:
- If max_tokens=6000 for a query:
  - Project docs get 60% → 3600 tokens
  - Shared context gets 40% → 2400 tokens
    - MANDATORY: 40% of 2400 = 960 tokens
    - BEST_PRACTICES: 30% = 720 tokens
    - GUIDELINES: 20% = 480 tokens
    - REFERENCE: 10% = 240 tokens

Ways to influence allocation (documented):
- Filter which shared categories you request via rlm_shared_context(categories=[...]).
- Link collections to projects and set ProjectSharedContext overrides (e.g., tokenBudgetPercent, enabledCategories).
- Use document/collection priority to surface higher-priority shared docs first.

Short answer
- By default Snipara splits the query token budget between project docs and shared context 60% / 40%.
- The shared-context portion is then allocated across document categories by priority: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%.

Details / how to control it
- When you call rlm_context_query (or rlm_shared_context), Snipara automatically includes linked shared collections and applies the budget split (default 60% project / 40% shared).
- rlm_shared_context supports max_tokens and category filters (e.g., categories=["MANDATORY","BEST_PRACTICES"]); the API returns the numeric budget_allocation (tokens per category) so you can see the exact allocation.
- Projects can override defaults via ProjectSharedContext: tokenBudgetPercent on the project→collection link can change how much of the overall budget is reserved for that shared collection.
- Example: max_tokens=4000 → shared context gets 1600 tokens (40%); within that 1600, MANDATORY ≈ 640 (40%), BEST_PRACTICES ≈ 480 (30%), GUIDELINES ≈ 320 (20%), REFERENCE ≈ 160 (10%).

Notes / gotchas
- You can limit which categories are included to concentrate budget on higher-priority docs.
- rlm_shared_context returns the computed budget_allocation so you can verify exact token counts.

Short answer:
- When Snipara returns context for a project query it splits your token budget between the project's own documents and any shared context collections it inherits. By default that split is 60% project docs / 40% shared context.
- The shared-context portion is then distributed across the shared documents by category and priority: MANDATORY (40%), BEST_PRACTICES (30%), GUIDELINES (20%), REFERENCE (10%). rlm_shared_context returns a concrete budget_allocation (tokens per category) when you request it.

Details / how to control it
- rlm_context_query automatically includes shared context for the project and applies the 60/40 distribution when building the returned sections.
- If you want to preview or fetch only shared context you can call rlm_shared_context(categories=[...], max_tokens=NNN). The call returns:
  - the selected documents
  - total_tokens used
  - budget_allocation showing how many tokens were assigned to each shared-category (example in docs: MANDATORY 40% of the shared budget, etc.)
- Per-project overrides: a ProjectSharedContext link can set tokenBudgetPercent to override the default allocation for that collection (tokenBudgetPercent in the ProjectSharedContext record).
- Document-level ordering inside a category is influenced by the document's category and priority fields (priority 0–100) — higher priority docs are favored when the category's token slice is filled.

Example (from docs)
- rlm_shared_context(..., max_tokens=4000) returned budget_allocation with numeric tokens (e.g., MANDATORY: 1139, BEST_PRACTICES: 854, GUIDELINES: 569, REFERENCE: 285) — illustrating the category percentages applied to the requested token budget.

If you need exact behavior for a specific project (custom tokenBudgetPercent, which collections are linked, or to tune max_tokens), call rlm_shared_context or inspect ProjectSharedContext for that project.

  - The Snipara VS Code extension provides workspace document scanning and local tooling. For synchronous editing, combine the extension with standard collaborative tools (GitHub Live Share, Codespaces) — the docs mention the extension but do not claim it provides built‑in real‑time editing.
- Audit & safety
  - Document summaries, audit logs and Prisma versioning give traceability for collaborative changes.

1. Keep docs in a Git repo; enable project GitHub sync in Snipara so edits are indexed automatically.
2. Use shared context collections for team standards so everyone’s edits follow the same rules.

Key points:
- ~90% context reduction (e.g., from ~500K tokens to ~5K highly relevant tokens)
- LLM-agnostic — clients use their own LLM (no vendor lock-in)
- No LLM inference costs passed through (pure compute service → high margins)
- Token budgeting and optimized ranking/chunking so responses fit client token limits

Short answer
- Snipara enforces a hard token budget per context query (default max_tokens, e.g. 4000) and builds an optimized context payload that fits that budget by counting real tokens (not characters) and selecting the most relevant content until the token budget is exhausted.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. Input
   - You call the primary tool rlm_context_query with parameters like query, max_tokens, search_mode (keyword | semantic | hybrid), include_metadata, and optional session_id.

2. Search & ranking
   - The engine performs a keyword, semantic, or hybrid search to produce candidate document sections.
   - Candidates are ranked by relevance (relevance_score).

3. Accurate token counting
   - Tokens are counted using tiktoken with the cl100k_base encoder (GPT‑4/Claude compatible) to get accurate token sizes for each candidate section.

4. Greedy selection against the budget
   - The engine greedily selects the highest‑relevance sections first.
   - It sums each section’s token_count and includes a section only if adding it stays within the max_tokens budget.
   - If a section would exceed the remaining budget, the engine attempts a partial inclusion.

5. Smart truncation for partial sections
   - If only part of a section fits, the engine uses smart_truncate() to cut content to the remaining token allowance.
   - Truncation is done at sentence boundaries (split on . ! ?) and appends "..." to indicate cut content.
   - The section metadata marks truncated: true.

6. Session & system context
   - Session context can be included (documented as allowed up to ~20% of the budget).
   - System instructions may be auto‑injected as part of the context.

7. Response metadata
   - The tool returns a structured result with:
     - sections: list of {title, content, file, lines, relevance_score, token_count, truncated?}
     - total_tokens: tokens used
     - truncated: whether the overall result hit the budget
     - session_context (if any)
     - suggestions (recommendations like related docs)
   - Search diagnostics (search_time_ms, sections_scanned, cache_hit) are returned in usage/ToolResult.

Budget allocation & higher‑level policies
- There are category allocation defaults (e.g., MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%) and a DEFAULT_ALLOCATION that favors project docs first.
- Allocation logic groups docs by category/source, computes per‑category budgets (fraction of total), and allocates within each category by relevance until that category’s budget is used.
- Hard limits are enforced (e.g., MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION) to cap total tokens in collections.

Additional features & tooling
- Token efficiency metrics are tracked (compression_ratio, token_reduction_pct, cost_savings_pct) — these are computed without calling an LLM.
- The system supports related recursive/multi‑query tools:
  - Decompose to split a complex query into subqueries (no LLM).
  - multi_query to run multiple context queries in parallel with per‑query budgets.
- Cost/tracking options exist (CompletionOptions example) to enforce token_budget and cost_budget.

Why this matters
- Real token counting (tiktoken) + greedy, relevance‑prioritized selection + sentence‑aware truncation preserves the most valuable context while guaranteeing the returned context will fit the model budget and reduce token/cost waste.

The selection is greedy by relevance and will partially include a section when only a portion fits, using sentence-aware truncation. Metadata (file, line ranges, relevance, token counts) and session context are returned alongside the optimized context.

Key pieces and behavior
- Token counting
  - Uses tiktoken (cl100k_base encoder) to get accurate token counts for content.
  - tiktoken>=0.5.0 is added to the server dependencies.

- Primary tool: rlm_context_query
  - Parameters: query (str), max_tokens (int, default 4000), search_mode (keyword|semantic|hybrid), include_metadata (bool).
  - Returns a structured result with sections (title, content, file, lines, relevance_score, token_count), total_tokens, truncated flag, session_context, and suggestions.
  - Also returns usage/diagnostics (search time, sections scanned, cache hit) in the tool response envelope.

- Selection algorithm (greedy token-budget selection)
  - Candidate sections are produced by keyword / semantic / hybrid search and ranked by relevance.
  - Iterate highest relevance → lowest. For each section, count its tokens.
  - If the full section fits within remaining tokens, include it.
  - If only part fits, apply smart_truncate() to cut content to fit the remaining tokens, mark the section as truncated, append "..." and stop (budget reached).
  - The response-level truncated flag is set when total selected tokens >= max_tokens.

- Smart truncation rules
  - Truncate to fit remaining tokens.
  - Prefer to cut at sentence boundaries (split on ., !, ?).
  - Add "..." marker for truncated content.
  - Indicate truncation in section metadata.

- Session & system context

  - System instructions are auto-injected where applicable.

  - Hard limits exist for collections and token counts per collection (e.g., MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION constant).

- Multi-query & decomposition support
  - Multi-query: distributed per-query budgets; each query runs its own rlm_context_query with its budget.
  - Decompose: breaks complex queries into subqueries (no LLM required) and estimates tokens per sub-query to build an execution plan.

- Metrics and tooling
  - Token-efficiency metrics (compression_ratio, token_reduction_pct, cost_savings_pct) measure optimization results without calling an LLM.

High-level summary — how Snipara manages token budgets

- Token counting
  - Uses tiktoken (cl100k_base encoder) to count tokens precisely (installed in the MCP server).
  - All candidate sections are tokenized to get accurate token counts before selection.

- Search & candidate generation
  - rlm_context_query supports search_mode: keyword | semantic | hybrid.
  - Candidates come from keyword search, semantic search, or a fused hybrid result.

- Ranking & selection (greedy, relevance-first)
  - Candidate sections are ranked by relevance.
  - Greedy selection: include highest‑relevance sections first until the token budget (max_tokens) would be exceeded.
  - If a section would overflow the remaining budget, a partial inclusion may be done using smart_truncate() to fit the remaining tokens.

- Smart truncation
  - Truncation happens at sentence boundaries (. ! ?) so content isn’t cut mid‑sentence.
  - Truncated content gets a "..." marker and section metadata includes truncated: true and the token_count used.

- Returned metadata
  - Each section includes title, content, file path, line ranges, relevance_score, token_count (and truncated flag when applicable).
  - The tool response includes sections[], total_tokens, truncated (overall), session_context (if any), and suggestions.

- Session & system context
  - Session context can be included (implementation notes mention up to ~20% of the budget for session context).
  - System instructions may be auto-injected into context where applicable.

- Budget allocation strategies (shared/project allocation)
  - There are default allocation presets used to split budgets across categories (examples in the docs):
    - CATEGORY_BUDGETS example: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%.
    - The web service DEFAULT_ALLOCATION example: project 40%, MANDATORY 25%, BEST_PRACTICES 20%, GUIDELINES 10%, REFERENCE 5%.
  - The front-end allocation logic groups documents and allocates per-category budgets, then selects documents within each sub‑budget (sorting by relevance).

- Related features & tooling
  - Multi-query support: distributes a total budget across multiple queries, calling the context query per-subquery.
  - Decompose tool: analyzes a complex query into subqueries and estimates token needs (no LLM).
  - Token efficiency metrics are tracked (compression_ratio, token_reduction_pct, cost_savings_pct) — these measure token savings without calling an LLM.
  - Hard limits and thresholds (e.g., min relevance threshold, per‑collection token limits) are enforced when allocating.

- Response shape (rlm_context_query)
  - The returned JSON includes structured sections with metadata plus summary fields (total_tokens, truncated, session_context, suggestions), and usage metrics (search time, sections scanned).

Example flow (conceptual)
1. Receive query + max_tokens.
2. Run keyword/semantic/hybrid search to produce candidate sections.
3. Rank by relevance.
4. Use tiktoken to get token counts and greedily add sections until budget exhausted.
5. If last section partially fits, smart_truncate at sentence boundary, mark truncated.
6. Return selected sections and metadata.

Short answer
- Shared Context Collections are reusable collections of team-wide documentation (coding standards, best practices, templates, reference) that admins upload once and then all linked projects automatically inherit.
- They let you enforce consistent rules (MANDATORY), recommended patterns (BEST_PRACTICES), guidelines, and reference material across your team’s projects and AI-assisted workflows.

How they work (overview)
1. Create a collection (team admin) — add documents describing standards, rules, templates.
2. Upload documents to the collection (e.g., rlm_upload_shared_document) and set each document’s category and priority.
3. Link the collection to one or more projects (dashboard or Project > Shared Contexts).
4. When querying project context or doing multi-project searches, the platform automatically merges shared collections with project docs and includes them in results (rlm_shared_context, rlm_context_query, rlm_multi_project_query).
5. The merged shared context is token-budgeted by category so higher-priority categories get more model attention (MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%).
6. Project-specific documents take precedence; linked collections can be reordered to adjust priority. Conflicts can be surfaced and must be acknowledged when linking.

Key tools / APIs
- rlm_list_collections: list available collections (team + public)
- rlm_upload_shared_document: add docs to a collection
- rlm_shared_context: retrieve merged shared context (supports category filters and max_tokens)
- rlm_context_query: project queries automatically include shared context
- rlm_multi_project_query: search across team projects
- rlm_list_templates / rlm_get_template: use prompt templates stored in collections
- rlm_inject: optionally inject shared context into a session

Important behavior & policies
- Documents are categorized by priority: MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE. Token budget is distributed 40/30/20/10 respectively.
- MANDATORY rules are non-negotiable and should always be followed.
- Collections can be public or team-scoped; collection metadata includes name, slug, scope, isPublic, isOfficial, usageCount, etc.
- Team plan is required for team-wide shared context features (not on Free/Pro).
- UI supports linking, reordering, conflict warnings, and removing collections.

Typical workflow example
- Admin uploads TypeScript coding standards (marking security rules as MANDATORY).
- Link the collection to all team projects.
- Developers call rlm_shared_context(categories=["MANDATORY","BEST_PRACTICES"]) or simply run rlm_context_query; the team rules are auto-injected and used by the model for subsequent completions, code generation, or code-review templates.

Best practices
- Start with clear MANDATORY rules.
- Keep documents focused (one topic per doc).
- Use priorities within categories (0–100) to indicate importance.
- Update collections as standards evolve and use templates for consistent reviews/specs.

Shared context collections are reusable bundles of team- or org‑level documentation (coding standards, best practices, guidelines, reference material) that you create once and automatically make available to all linked projects so the team’s AI-assisted workflows stay consistent.

How they work (overview)
- What they are:
  - Collections of shared documents (e.g., “TypeScript Coding Standards”) containing rules, patterns, templates, examples.
  - Stored as SharedContextCollection objects and can be GLOBAL, TEAM, or USER scoped and optionally public/official.
- Creation & linking:
  - Team admin creates a collection and uploads documents (e.g., via rlm_upload_shared_document).
  - Collections are linked to projects in the dashboard (Project > Shared Context → Link Collection).
  - Once linked, projects automatically inherit the collection’s documents.
- Document categories & token budgeting:
  - Documents are categorized (priority order): MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE.
  - Shared-context token budget is split by category by default (example: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%).
  - Individual documents also have a numeric priority (0–100) used to rank importance within a category (higher = more important).
- Runtime usage:
  - rlm_shared_context returns the merged shared context for linked collections (can filter categories, set max_tokens, include_project_docs).
  - Shared context is merged with project docs when you run rlm_context_query; the platform distributes tokens between project docs and shared context (example distribution shown: 60% project docs + 40% shared context).
  - You can inject shared context into a session (rlm_inject) so it applies to subsequent queries and completions.
- Tools & integrations:
  - rlm_list_collections, rlm_list_templates, rlm_get_template, rlm_multi_project_query are provided to list collections/templates, fetch templates, and search across projects.
  - Templates from collections (code-review, security-review, etc.) can be retrieved and populated with variables.
  - Multi-project queries let you search implementations across all team projects.
- Behavior & precedence:
  - Projects inherit linked collections; project-specific documents take precedence over shared docs.
  - Collections can be ordered (UI drag-and-drop) to control priority between multiple linked collections.
  - Conflict handling: linking can surface conflicts that must be acknowledged or resolved.
- Access & plans:
  - Team plan (or Enterprise) is required for team-wide shared collections and related features. Some enterprise-only features exist (custom categories, analytics).

Typical workflow (simple)
1. Create a shared collection and upload rules (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, etc.).
2. Link the collection to one or more projects.
3. At development start, fetch shared context (rlm_shared_context) and inject it into the session (rlm_inject) or let rlm_context_query include it automatically.
4. Implement features / run completions; the AI will follow the team’s shared rules.

High-level summary

- Snipara implements the standard OAuth 2.0 Device Flow (RFC 8628) for MCP authentication so users can authenticate a CLI without copying API keys.
- User flow: user runs a CLI command → CLI shows a URL and a short code for the user to enter in a browser → user authenticates in the browser.
- The CLI (device client) polls the server’s device token endpoint until the user finishes authentication.

Key technical details from Snipara docs

- Device token polling endpoint: POST /api/oauth/device/token
  - Example request body:
    {
      "grant_type": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code",
      "device_code": "abc123...",
      "client_id": "snipara_cli"
    }
  - While the user hasn’t completed auth the endpoint returns:
    { "error": "authorization_pending" }
  - On success the endpoint returns an OAuth token bundle:
    {
      "access_token": "snipara_at_...",
      "token_type": "Bearer",
      "expires_in": 2592000,
      "refresh_token": "snipara_rt_..."
    }

Token format and validation

- Access tokens use the prefix snipara_at_.
- Tokens are validated by a DB lookup plus an expiry check (validate_oauth_token()) and scopes (read/write) are enforced.

Client behavior and fallbacks

- The client first looks for a stored OAuth token (~/ .snipara/tokens.json). If present it is used.
- If no valid OAuth token is available, the client falls back to API key authentication (X-API-Key: rlm_...).
- To force API-key-only behavior you can:
  - Set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the MCP client env to skip ~/.snipara/tokens.json and force the API key.
  - Or remove/rename ~/.snipara/tokens.json (e.g., rm or mv).
- If an OAuth token is invalid or expired the server returns HTTP 401 with the message shown in docs:
  "Invalid or expired OAuth token. Re-authenticate at https://snipara.com/dashboard or run /snipara:quickstart"

Location of device-flow endpoints

- Device-flow endpoints are under the /api/auth/device/ endpoints (standard device-flow implementation).

Snipara uses a strict layered architecture with one-way imports. The layers (lower layers cannot import from higher) are:

- Routes (app/api/) — top-level HTTP route handlers
- Services (lib/services/) — external API adapters and business logic integrations
- Repositories (lib/db/queries/) — centralized CRUD/database access (repository pattern)
- Utils & Types — pure utilities and types with no dependencies

Route handlers are thin (authenticate, validate via Zod, delegate to service/query, return response) and all DB access must go through the repository layer.

Short answer
- Snipara will extract a small, highly relevant subset of your 500K-token codebase (the docs show an example reduced to ~5K tokens) so the context you send to the LLM fits easily inside a 128K window.
- That reduction translates to roughly a 100× compression (500,000 / 5,000) and ≈99% fewer tokens sent — the same percentage reduction roughly equals the percent reduction in LLM token costs (Snipara reports compression_ratio, token_reduction_pct and cost_savings_pct).

How Snipara achieves this (from the docs)
- Token budgeting: you specify max_tokens (e.g., set <= 128K). The engine greedily chooses the highest-relevance sections until the token budget is used.
- Smart truncation: if only part of a section fits, it truncates at sentence boundaries and marks it truncated.
- Relevance ranking / search modes: keyword, semantic, or hybrid search to find the best sections.
- Session context: previous conversation/compact context can be included (up to 20% of the budget per implementation notes).
- Metadata: returned sections include file, line ranges, relevance scores, token counts, and suggestions.

Numeric example (from the docs)
- Original corpus: 500,000 tokens.
- Optimized context (example in docs): ~5,000 tokens.
- Compression ratio = 500,000 / 5,000 = 100×.
- Token reduction = (1 - 5,000 / 500,000) = 0.99 → 99% fewer tokens.
- cost_savings_pct ≈ 99% (Snipara exposes this metric; actual $ savings depend on your LLM provider’s per-token pricing).

Why this matters with a 128K window
- 500K tokens cannot be placed into a 128K context window. Snipara’s optimized ~5K token context fits comfortably within 128K, meaning you can:
  - Send the relevant context to the model without losing important information to truncation.
  - Reduce the number of tokens billed by your LLM provider by roughly the token_reduction_pct reported.

What you’ll get from Snipara in practice
- An output payload that fits your specified max_tokens and includes the selected sections plus metadata and suggestions.
- Metrics (compression_ratio, token_reduction_pct, cost_savings_pct) so you can quantify savings; actual dollar savings require your provider’s per-token rates.

- Snipara compacts a 500K‑token codebase down to a few thousand relevant tokens (the docs cite reducing 500K → ~5K), so the LLM only receives the useful context that fits inside your 128K window.

How it helps (mechanics)
- Token budgeting: you set a max_tokens budget (e.g., the portion of the 128K window you want to use for context). Snipara greedily selects the highest‑relevance sections until that budget is filled.

- Metadata & control: returned sections include file path, line ranges, relevance scores and token counts so you know exactly what was sent to the LLM.
- Session context & system injection: session context can be included (docs note up to 20% of the budget) and system instructions are auto‑injected.
- LLM‑agnostic & no inference by Snipara: Snipara does the selection/compaction; you still call your LLM provider — Snipara itself doesn’t run inference.

- Because LLM API cost is proportional to tokens processed, cutting the context by orders of magnitude reduces your LLM token spend by roughly the same factor. Snipara does not add LLM inference costs itself (you only pay your LLM for the reduced payload and the model response).

- You have a 500K token repo but your model window is 128K. Without compaction you can’t send everything. With Snipara you might request a context budget (e.g., 100K tokens) and Snipara will return the top sections that fit (or a much smaller ~5K set per the docs), letting you keep room in the 128K window for the prompt and response.

Short answer
- Use rlm_context_query for almost all documentation Q&A and single-topic lookups.

Why and when (details)
- rlm_context_query (primary tool)
  - Full-featured semantic search with token budgeting — fast, single-call, includes shared best-practices first.
  - Best for single questions or any query with a clear, single-topic answer (e.g., "How does authentication work?").
- rlm_decompose (power-user tool)
  - Breaks a complex, multi-part question into sub-queries you can inspect/adjust.
  - Use when you need to research or implement a multi-step feature (e.g., "Implement user authentication with email verification") and want separate context for each part.
  - Typical workflow: rlm_decompose -> review/modify subQueries -> rlm_multi_query (query each sub-topic in parallel).

Rule of thumb
- Default: run rlm_context_query first.
- If the answer space is multi-topic or you want finer control and parallel retrieval, decompose then multi-query.

Use rlm_context_query for single-step documentation/code lookups and use rlm_decompose when the question is complex and you want to break it into controllable sub-queries.

Quick guidelines
- rlm_context_query
  - Use for typical documentation Q&A, code lookups, and any query that has a clear, single answer.

  - Examples: “Where is auth handled?”, “How does authentication work?”, “List API endpoints.”

- rlm_decompose
  - Use when the task is multi-part or complex and you want to inspect/modify the sub-queries before running them.
  - Works well as the first step in a multi-step workflow (decompose → run sub-queries with rlm_multi_query → combine results).
  - Examples: “Implement user authentication with email verification” (decompose into login flow, JWT handling, email service, verification flow).

Suggested workflow for complex tasks
1. rlm_decompose(query) → get subQueries
2. Optionally edit/approve subQueries
3. rlm_multi_query({queries: subQueries}) to fetch context in parallel
4. Combine and synthesize results

If your question is simple and direct, call rlm_context_query first. If it’s complex and you want manual control over how it’s split and queried, start with rlm_decompose.

Which to use (mapping)

- General documentation search or “best practices” → hybrid
- API endpoint discovery or code pattern lookup → keyword
- Error code lookup → keyword
- Best-effort/fuzzy matches where both exact terms and semantics matter → hybrid

Available authentication options (from the docs) and how they differ in security:

1) API Key authentication
- How it is used:
  - Header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
  - Format: keys begin with the rlm_ prefix.
  - Lookup: server SHA-256-hashes the provided key and looks up the hash in the ApiKey table (project keys tried first, then team keys).
  - Response metadata: auth info includes user_id, access_level and key metadata; UI/logs show only the first 8 chars of the key.
- Security characteristics:
  - Keys are stored hashed (SHA-256) in the DB.
  - Simple to use and supported by both MCP and Inject endpoints (Inject requires X-API-Key; MCP accepts X-API-Key or Bearer).
  - Access control via the key’s access_level; revocation and visibility are managed via the dashboard (keys are shown partially).
  - Long-lived keys can be convenient but may be a higher-risk if exposed unless rotated/revoked.

2) OAuth 2.0 tokens (Device Flow)
- How it is used:
  - Header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
  - Token format: access tokens start with snipara_at_ (implementation reference).
  - Device flow (RFC 8628): CLI/device gets device_code, user authenticates in browser; token polling via /api/oauth/device/token.
  - Tokens include access and refresh tokens (examples show access response with access_token, refresh_token, token_type, expires_in).
  - Tokens are listed via GET /api/settings/tokens and can be revoked with DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[tokenId].
- Security characteristics:
  - Tokens (access and refresh) are stored as hashes in the DB and include fields for accessExpiresAt and refreshExpiresAt.
  - OAuth supports scopes (e.g., "mcp:read mcp:write") and the server enforces scope-based access.
  - Tokens expire and can be refreshed; expiration reduces exposure window versus non-expiring keys.
  - Device flow is designed to avoid copying API keys into devices and is generally safer for interactive authentication.
  - Revocation is supported (DELETE token endpoint) and tokens can be enumerated (GET tokens).
  - If a token is expired or revoked, requests receive a 401 with instructions to re-authenticate.

3) Endpoint-specific requirements / transport differences
- MCP endpoint (api.snipara.com/mcp/{slug}) accepts either:
  - X-API-Key: rlm_... OR
  - Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
- Inject API endpoint (www.snipara.com/api/projects/{slug}/automation/inject) accepts:
  - X-API-Key only.
- Implication: For some flows you must use an API key (Inject API); MCP can accept OAuth or API key.

Operational/security notes from the docs
- Local token storage: OAuth tokens are kept in ~/.snipara/tokens.json. If present, the MCP client/server prefers OAuth tokens over API keys.
- Caching: The MCP server process may cache OAuth credentials. Removing ~/.snipara/tokens.json may not be sufficient until Claude/the server is fully restarted.
- You can force API-key usage by removing or backing up tokens.json and restarting the client/server.
- Invalid/expired tokens return a 401 with guidance to re-authenticate.

Summary (security trade-offs)
- OAuth (device flow) gives stronger short-window security: scoped tokens, expiry/refresh, revocation endpoints, and avoids copying raw API keys — better for interactive/login flows.
- API keys are hashed in storage and provide access control, are simple and required for some APIs, but can be longer-lived and potentially riskier if not rotated/revoked promptly.
- Choose OAuth where you need fine-grained scopes, expirations, revocability and safer user login; choose API keys where automated systems/inject endpoints require them or when simpler credentialing is necessary.

Authentication options (from the docs)
1) API Key authentication
- Header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
- Key format: rlm_ prefix
- Server-side: client-sent key is hashed with SHA-256 and compared against ApiKey table (project keys checked first, then team keys).
- Storage/visibility: hashed in DB; UI/logs only show first 8 chars of the key.
- Endpoints: accepted by MCP endpoints and required by Inject API (Inject API uses X-API-Key only).
- Notes: No expiration is specified in the docs (i.e., treated as persistent until rotated/revoked outside these docs).

Security characteristics:
- Strong server-side storage practice (SHA-256 hashed).
- Persistent keys — if leaked they remain valid until revoked/rotated.
- Simpler for automated/server-to-server use; must be protected at rest on clients.

2) OAuth 2.0 tokens (Device Flow)
- Header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
- Token format: snipara_at_ prefix for access tokens; response includes access_token, expires_in, refresh_token.
- Flow: RFC 8628 device flow — CLI shows code and URL; user authenticates in browser.
- Server-side storage: access and refresh tokens are stored as hashes (OAuthToken with accessTokenHash, refreshTokenHash, tokenPrefix, scope, clientName, accessExpiresAt, refreshExpiresAt).
- Token lifecycle: access tokens have expiry (example expires_in: 2,592,000 s = 30 days in the example); refresh tokens exist and can be used to obtain new access tokens.
- Endpoints: MCP server accepts Bearer tokens; Inject API requires X-API-Key.
- Management: tokens can be listed (GET /api/settings/tokens) and revoked (DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[tokenId]).

Security characteristics:
- Shorter-lived access tokens reduce exposure window; refresh tokens support rotation/refresh.
- Device flow avoids embedding long-lived API keys in the CLI and avoids copying API keys into browsers — described as “secure” for MCP authentication.
- Tokens are hashed in DB (good storage practice); tokenPrefix (first 12 chars) used for identification instead of exposing full token.

Other relevant behaviors and operational notes from the docs
- Priority: MCP server will check for a stored OAuth token in ~/.snipara/tokens.json first, then fall back to API key from environment variables. This affects which credential is used automatically.
- Forcing API-key usage: remove or move ~/.snipara/tokens.json (rm or mv) and restart Claude Code to avoid cached OAuth credentials.
- Caching: even after removing tokens.json, an MCP server process may cache OAuth credentials — fully exit and rerun the process to clear that.
- Expired/invalid OAuth tokens yield HTTP 401 with guidance to re-authenticate (message example included in docs).

Summary comparison (high-level)
- OAuth (device flow): better for interactive CLI auth, short-lived access tokens, refresh tokens, token revocation/listing available, hashed storage — generally better for limiting long-term exposure.
- API key: persistent credentials, SHA-256 hashed in DB, suitable for automated use but if compromised remain valid until revoked/rotated (no expiry noted in docs). Use with care and store securely.
